rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe but common cutaneous candidal intertrigo in a poorly-controlled diabetic, precipitated by recent broad-spectrum antibiotics. The patient is systemically well and afebrile with the source's own cellulitis screen negative (no spreading induration, no localised heat of deep cellulitis, no lymphadenopathy), so this is neither an emergency (not T4/T5) nor an urgent same-day problem (not T3). It is telehealth/GP-manageable with drying, a topical antifungal, symptom relief and glycaemic optimisation. Because the diabetes plus broken, weeping skin genuinely raises infection risk AND telehealth cannot palpate the folds to exclude early cellulitis, the disposition is PROMPT in-person GP review (T2, within 24-72 hours) rather than pure self-care (T0/T1) — and prominent mandatory escalation edges are attached for secondary bacterial cellulitis/sepsis. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED here is over-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness spreading well beyond the skin folds, skin becoming hot and hard, fever or rigors, or thick/foul-smelling pus
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day in-person / emergency assessment if any of these develop
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates a secondary bacterial cellulitis of the broken diabetic fold skin that needs systemic antibiotics and in-person (possibly hospital) assessment — a complication telehealth cannot palpate for.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness spreads well beyond the creases, the skin turns hot and hard, you get a fever or the shivers, or the fluid becomes thick and smelly, get seen the same day — go to an emergency department if you can't be seen quickly.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe pain out of proportion, rapidly spreading dusky/darkening skin, skin breakdown, or feeling very unwell/collapsing
escalation_edges.rationale: These are warning features of a necrotising soft-tissue infection or systemic sepsis — a surgical/medical emergency, especially in a diabetic, needing an ambulance and immediate hospital care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes far worse than the rash looks, the skin rapidly darkens or breaks down, or you feel very unwell, faint or feverish and shivery, call 000 straight away and tell them you have diabetes and a spreading skin infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No improvement after 3-5 days of treatment, worsening despite it, or high blood-sugar readings and feeling generally unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Prompt GP review within 24-48 hours if not improving
escalation_edges.rationale: A candidal intertrigo that fails to settle may be misdiagnosed (e.g. dermatophyte), may have a secondary infection, or reflects ongoing poor glycaemic control needing review and possibly a swab or different treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it hasn't started improving within 3-5 days, or your blood sugars are running high and you feel off, book a prompt GP review to check the diagnosis and your diabetes.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Baseline: severe but systemically-well candidal intertrigo of the folds in a poorly-controlled diabetic, cellulitis screen negative
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Start drying + antifungal now and arrange prompt in-person GP review within 24-72 hours
escalation_edges.rationale: The proportionate disposition once emergencies are excluded — begin patient-performable treatment immediately and be seen in person soon because the diabetes and broken skin cannot be safely assessed for early infection over telehealth.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Start keeping the folds dry and using an antifungal cream now, and arrange to be seen in person by your GP within the next day or two so they can check the skin and your diabetes.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Poorly-controlled type 2 diabetes with a significant skin barrier breach
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for in-person review and for acting on the secondary-infection triggers — infection develops faster and heals slower in poorly-controlled diabetes; ensure glycaemic control is reviewed as part of management.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adult with obesity/deep skin folds and temporary immobility from friction pain
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm the patient can manage self-care and get to prompt in-person review despite being bed-bound with pain; arrange practical support if mobility or reaching the folds is limited, and lower the threshold to attend in person if they cannot manage.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a common yeast (fungal) infection of the skin folds — treatable and not an emergency. Right now, gently pat the folds completely dry (a cool hair-dryer helps the deep creases), wear loose clothing, and start an antifungal cream that also calms inflammation twice a day, with a barrier paste over the top to protect the raw skin. Because you have diabetes and the skin is broken, arrange to be seen in person by your GP within the next day or two to check the skin and your diabetes. Do NOT use a strong steroid cream on its own and you don't need antibiotics unless an infection develops.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a prompt in-person GP appointment (within 24-72 hours); if after hours use an after-hours GP or 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for advice
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If this is after hours you do not need an emergency department for the rash itself — start the drying and antifungal measures and see your GP in the next day or two. Only seek same-day/emergency care if a red-flag develops: spreading hot hard redness, fever/rigors, thick foul-smelling pus, or severe worsening pain (call 000 if you feel very unwell or the skin is rapidly darkening/breaking down).
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Tell the GP the rash started ~5 days ago and flared over 48 hours, that it followed a 10-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate, and note your recent blood-sugar control and current metformin dose; mention any home temperature readings.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether there is early secondary bacterial cellulitis in the broken diabetic fold skin — telehealth cannot palpate for heat, induration or tenderness beyond the visible redness.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the uncertainty as a reason for prompt in-person review plus explicit secondary-infection safety-netting, while starting patient-performable antifungal/drying measures now — NOT as a reason to default to 000/ED when the patient is systemically well.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismissing the diabetic broken skin with pure self-care and no follow-up (under-action), or escalating a systemically-well patient with a negative cellulitis screen straight to the emergency department / 000 (over-escalation).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as trivial with pure self-care and no clinician review or safety-net, in a poorly-controlled diabetic with broken weeping skin.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An unrecognised early secondary cellulitis progresses to systemic sepsis before anyone lays eyes on the skin or advises when to escalate.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising only a routine (1-2 week) GP appointment with no secondary-infection safety-net, delaying review of a diabetic with broken fold skin.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a systemically-well patient with a negative cellulitis screen to urgent same-day care, the emergency department, or calling 000 for a common candidal intertrigo — unnecessary ED load and patient distress/cost when prompt in-person GP review is proportionate.
